summary:
The early royal tombs at Abydos, located on the western edge of the Nile Valley in Upper Egypt approximately 500 kilometers south of Cairo, provide the primary archaeological evidence for the earliest phase of Egyptian kingship during the [[event:1635|Naqada III period]] and the [[event:1639|First Dynasty]]. From around 3200 BCE onward, Abydos served as the burial ground for Egypt's proto-dynastic and early dynastic rulers, and the tombs found there — excavated principally by Flinders Petrie in the 1890s and by German archaeologists in the late twentieth and early twenty-first centuries — have yielded crucial evidence for the origins of Egyptian royal ideology, funerary practice, and writing.

The earliest tombs at the site labeled Cemetery U by archaeologists include the tomb U-j, dated to approximately 3200-3150 BCE and attributed to a king tentatively identified as Scorpion I. Tomb U-j is extraordinary for its size (twelve linked chambers), the large quantity of imported commodities found within it (including wine jars from the Levant and Palestinian region), and its yielding of some of the earliest known hieroglyphic inscriptions — small bone and ivory tags bearing simple signs designating geographic regions and quantities, attached to goods in the tomb as inventory labels.

The later First Dynasty tombs at Abydos, from the reign of Narmer through Qa'a, grew increasingly elaborate, with mudbrick-lined burial chambers surrounded by subsidiary graves containing sacrificed retainers and animals. The proximity of the tombs to the ancient cult center of Osiris at Abydos contributed to the city's sacred status, and Abydos remained a pilgrimage center and burial site of religious significance throughout the pharaonic period, even as later kings were buried elsewhere.
